steps.title: Identify what the experiment actually showed
steps.content:
In the pear juice test, the well containing E. coli O157:H7 turned purple while all other wells (containing other E. coli strains or other pathogens) turned pink. This means the aptamers bound to their target and did not bind to non-targets — they were specific.
  • Choice A — aptamer-based tests in real-world foods are more likely to show a high degree of specificity if the focal pathogen is E. coli O157:H7 than if the focal pathogen is another strain of E. coli or another species.: compares O157:H7-targeting aptamers to aptamers targeting other pathogens — the experiment only tested O157:H7-targeting aptamers, so this comparison is unsupported ✗
  • Choice B — uncertainty about the specificity of aptamer-based tests for pathogens in real-world foods may be due to the similarity between E. coli O157:H7 and other E. coli strains.: the results resolved specificity rather than raising new uncertainty about it ✗
  • Choice C — the specificity of the tests in a real-world food was unaffected by the aptamers' tendency to bind to different strains of E. coli.: the aptamers did not bind to other strains; this misrepresents the results ✗
  • Choice D — the aptamers successfully bound to E. coli O157:H7 and the tests displayed a high degree of specificity in a real-world food.: directly describes the purple color result (binding to target) and the pink results for non-targets (high specificity) ✓

steps.title: Confirm the answer
steps.content: The purple well confirms target binding; the pink wells confirm no non-target binding — together they demonstrate high specificity in real-world pear juice. D is correct.
